title: Conjunctions — že, protože, když
description: Master four essential Czech conjunctions for building complex sentences: že (that — reporting speech/thoughts), protože (because — giving reasons), když (when — for factual/recurring events), and jestli (if/whether — expressing uncertainty). Learn how word order changes in subordinate clauses and practice connecting ideas naturally.

exercises.prompt: What does the conjunction 'že' mean in Czech?

data.question_en: What does the conjunction 'že' mean in Czech?
data.explanation: 'Že' = that. It introduces a subordinate clause reporting what someone thinks, says, knows, etc. Example: 'Myslím, že je to pravda.' = I think that it is true. 'Že' is one of the most common Czech conjunctions.

exercises.prompt: Complete with 'že': 'I know that you are right.'

data.explanation: 'Vím, že máš pravdu.' = I know that you are right. The conjunction 'že' introduces the subordinate clause. Note the comma before 'že' — Czech always uses a comma before že, protože, když, jestli, and other subordinating conjunctions.

'Protože' = because. It gives a reason or cause. Example: 'Nešel jsem ven, protože pršelo.' = I didn't go out because it was raining. The subordinate clause with 'protože' can come after or before the main clause.

exercises.prompt: Complete with 'protože': 'He stayed home because he was sick.'

data.explanation: 'Zůstal doma, protože byl nemocný.' = He stayed home because he was sick. 'Protože' always introduces the reason. Word order: main clause + comma + protože + subordinate clause. The subordinate clause keeps normal subject-verb order.

exercises.prompt: When do you use 'když' (as opposed to 'až' for future)?

data.question_en: When do you use 'když' (as opposed to 'až' for future)?
options: For factual, recurring, or real situations
data.explanation: 'Když' = when — for factual, recurring, or generally true events. Example: 'Když prší, zůstávám doma.' = When it rains, I stay home (generally/recurring). Compare with 'až' which is 'when' for one-time future events: 'Až přijdeš, zavolej.' = When you arrive (in the future), call me.

exercises.answer_key: For factual, recurring, or real situations

exercises.prompt: Complete with 'když': 'When the sun shines, we go outside.'

data.explanation: 'Když svítí slunce, chodíme ven.' = When the sun shines, we go outside (a recurring/general situation). 'Když' is used for factual 'when' — things that happen regularly or are generally true. The když-clause can start the sentence (with comma) or follow the main clause.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'if/whether' (expressing uncertainty) in Czech?

data.question_en: How do you say 'if/whether' (expressing uncertainty) in Czech?
data.explanation: 'Jestli' = if/whether — used for indirect questions and expressing uncertainty. Example: 'Nevím, jestli přijde.' = I don't know if/whether he will come. Not to be confused with 'kdyby' (if — hypothetical) or 'jestliže' (if — conditional, more formal).

exercises.prompt: Complete with 'jestli': 'I will ask if he has time.'

data.explanation: 'Zeptám se, jestli má čas.' = I will ask if/whether he has time. 'Jestli' introduces indirect yes/no questions. The clause after 'jestli' uses normal indicative word order, not question word order. This is different from direct questions.

exercises.prompt: Arrange the words into a correct sentence: 'I think that this film is good.'

data.explanation: 'Myslím, že ten film je dobrý.' = I think that this film is good. After 'že', the subordinate clause keeps normal word order (subject–verb–complement). Czech always uses a comma before 'že'. The main clause 'Myslím' comes first.

exercises.answer_key: Myslím, že ten film je dobrý.

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'She says that she is tired.'

data.source: She says that she is tired.
data.grammar_note: 'Říká, že je unavená.' = She says that she is tired. 'Říká' = she says (3rd person sing. of 'říkat'). 'Že' introduces the reported speech. 'Je unavená' = she is tired (feminine -á ending agrees with female subject). The pronoun 'ona' can be omitted — the verb ending already indicates the subject.

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct conjunction: že, protože, když, or jestli?

data.sentence: Nepřišel do práce, ___ byl nemocný. Vím, ___ to není pravda. Nevím, ___ zítra přijde.
data.explanation: The three sentences use three different conjunctions: 1) 'protože' (because — giving a reason), 2) 'že' (that — reporting what you know), 3) 'jestli' (if/whether — expressing uncertainty). 'Nepřišel do práce, protože byl nemocný. Vím, že to není pravda. Nevím, jestli zítra přijde.'

exercises.prompt: Pronounce the complex sentence using multiple conjunctions.

data.target_text: Myslím, že je to dobrý nápad, protože když pracujeme spolu, všechno je lehčí.
data.translation_en: I think it's a good idea, because when we work together, everything is easier.

exercises.answer_key: Myslím, že je to dobrý nápad, protože když pracujeme spolu, všechno je lehčí.
